text: A seventy-four years old female patient presented to our clinic complaining of full thickness rectal prolapse and obstructive defecation. Eight centimeters prolapse bowel segment with segmental ischemia, ulceration and distal rectal stricture approximately two centimeters during examination was found. During colonoscopy, no other pathology was detected. Past medical history consisted of ischemic cardiac disease, type two diabetes and Alzheimer's disease.
The patient is positioned in lithotomy, with the renal area exposed using a lone star retractor. The prolapsed rectum is fully averted, revealing fibrotic tissues and a distal rectal stricture.
The surgeon makes a circumferential incision in the rectal wall using monopolar electrocautery, positioned approximately one centimeter distal from the dentate line.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the roll-up segment of the rectum using monopolar electrocautery, applying bipolar hemostasis as needed, and ligating the mesorectal vessels with 3-0 Vicryl sutures. The rectal wall proximal to the dentate line is divided circumferentially, followed by division of the mesorectum at the rectal transduction line.

The surgeon incises the anterior wall of the prolapsed rectal segment and places three full-thickness interrupted coloanastomotic sutures using free O Vicryl. The distal part of the prolapsing rectum is fully transected, followed by complete anastomosis using suturing technique.
Postoperative course was uneventful, patient discharged on postoperative day four.
